name: Fluid Dynamics
description: Navier-Stokes, turbulence, boundary layers, computational fluid dynamics

results.name: Navier-Stokes Equations
results.structural_conclusion: Fluid motion is fundamentally governed by the conservation of momentum coupled with viscous dissipation, creating a nonlinear partial differential equation system that encodes all possible fluid behaviors.
results.source_citation: Stokes, G.G. (1845). Trans. Cambridge Phil. Soc. 8, 287-319

results.name: Reynolds Number Similarity
results.structural_conclusion: Fluid flows are fundamentally organized by the ratio of inertial to viscous forces, with this single dimensionless parameter determining flow regime transitions and similarity classes.
results.source_citation: Reynolds, O. (1883). Phil. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. 174, 935-982

results.name: Kolmogorov -5/3 Law
results.structural_conclusion: Turbulent energy cascades through scales following a universal power law in the inertial range, revealing that turbulence has a self-similar structure independent of specific flow conditions.
results.source_citation: Kolmogorov, A.N. (1941). Dokl. Akad. Nauk SSSR 30, 301-305

results.name: Prandtl Boundary Layer Theory
results.structural_conclusion: High Reynolds number flows naturally separate into inviscid outer regions and thin viscous boundary layers, fundamentally decomposing the flow field into distinct dynamical zones.
results.source_citation: Prandtl, L. (1904). Verhandlungen des III Int. Math. Kongr., Heidelberg, 484-491

results.name: Kutta-Joukowski Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Lift generation is fundamentally tied to circulation around a body, establishing that force production in inviscid flows requires topological flow structures.

results.name: Taylor-Couette Instability
results.structural_conclusion: Rotating flows undergo a hierarchy of symmetry-breaking instabilities, revealing that fluid systems spontaneously generate ordered structures through competition between centrifugal and viscous forces.
results.source_citation: Taylor, G.I. (1923). Phil. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. A 223, 289-343

results.name: Helmholtz Vortex Theorems
results.structural_conclusion: Vorticity in inviscid flows obeys topological conservation laws, with vortex lines behaving as material lines that cannot be created or destroyed within the fluid.
results.source_citation: Helmholtz, H. (1858). J. Reine Angew. Math. 55, 25-55

results.name: Blasius Boundary Layer Solution
results.structural_conclusion: Laminar boundary layers possess self-similar structure, with the velocity profile collapsing to a universal form when properly scaled.

results.name: von Kármán Vortex Street
results.structural_conclusion: Bluff body wakes spontaneously organize into periodic vortex patterns with specific spacing ratios, demonstrating that wake flows have preferred structural configurations.
results.source_citation: von Kármán, T. (1911). Nachr. Ges. Wiss. Göttingen, Math.-Phys. Kl., 509-517

results.name: Rayleigh-Bénard Convection
results.structural_conclusion: Thermal convection self-organizes into cellular patterns when buoyancy forces overcome viscous damping, revealing that fluid systems spontaneously break symmetry to maximize heat transport.

results.name: Kelvin's Circulation Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Circulation around material loops is conserved in inviscid barotropic flows, establishing that fluid motion preserves certain integral quantities tied to flow topology.
results.source_citation: Thomson, W. (1869). Trans. R. Soc. Edinburgh 25, 217-260

results.name: Stokes' Law for Sphere Drag
results.structural_conclusion: At low Reynolds numbers, fluid forces scale linearly with velocity and viscosity, revealing a regime where inertia is negligible and dynamics are reversible.
results.source_citation: Stokes, G.G. (1851). Trans. Cambridge Phil. Soc. 9, 8-106

results.name: Bernoulli's Principle
results.structural_conclusion: Energy conservation along streamlines creates an inverse relationship between pressure and velocity, fundamentally constraining how kinetic and potential energy are distributed in steady flows.

results.name: Orr-Sommerfeld Equation
results.structural_conclusion: Linear stability of parallel flows is governed by a fourth-order eigenvalue problem, revealing that flow stability depends on the competition between destabilizing inertia and stabilizing viscosity.
results.source_citation: Sommerfeld, A. (1908). Atti del IV Congresso Internazionale dei Matematici, Rome, 3, 116-124

results.name: Richardson Energy Cascade
results.structural_conclusion: Turbulent energy transfers from large to small scales through a hierarchy of eddies, establishing that turbulence has a multiscale structure with directional energy flow.
results.source_citation: Richardson, L.F. (1922). Weather Prediction by Numerical Process, Cambridge University Press

structural_conclusion: Fluid dynamics reveals that continuous media spontaneously organize into discrete structures (vortices, boundary layers, convection cells) through symmetry-breaking instabilities and nonlinear interactions. The field's fundamental results show that flows are governed by conservation laws that couple with dissipation to create universal scaling behaviors, self-similar patterns, and topological constraints that persist across vastly different flow regimes.
